item.topic: rain at dusk on a tin roof

haiku_bare.text:
Gray rain on tin roof
Each drop a note of twilight
Dusk's gentle music

haiku_cascade.text:
Dusk rain taps the roof
Tin drums echo through twilight
Into quiet night

meta.surface_draft:
Dusk rain taps the roof
Tin drums echo through twilight
Into quiet night
meta.surface_revision:
Dusk rain strikes the roof
Tin screams like a living bell
Till night becomes sound

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Dusk falls, rain begins
Tin roof sings its ancient song
Rhythm of the night

meta.surface_draft:
Dusk falls, rain begins
Tin roof sings its ancient song
Rhythm of the night

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
Rain taps the tin roof—
Each drop rings bright as a star—
Night swallows the sound

meta.surface_draft:
Dusk rain patters down
Tin roof sings its ancient song
Day fades to silence
meta.surface_revision:
Rain taps the tin roof—
Each drop rings bright as a star—
Night swallows the sound

item.topic: an autumn leaf settling on a still pond

haiku_bare.text:
Red leaf descends slow
Ripples fade on mirror glass
Silence holds it still

haiku_cascade.text:
Autumn leaf descends
Landing on still water's face
Ripples fade to peace

meta.surface_draft:
Autumn leaf descends
Landing on still water's face
Ripples fade to peace
meta.surface_revision:
Bronze leaf settles down
On still water's glassed surface
Deepens into dusk

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Brown leaf floats on down
Gentle ripples break the calm
Still pond now alive

meta.surface_draft:
Brown leaf floats on down
Gentle ripples break the calm
Still pond now alive

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
Rust-red maple leaf
Settles like a sigh released
the pond breathes it in

meta.surface_draft:
Autumn leaf descends
Touches the still pond's surface
Ripples mark its rest
meta.surface_revision:
Rust-red maple leaf
Settles like a sigh released
the pond breathes it in

item.topic: the silence after a long argument

haiku_bare.text:
The silence after a long argument

You turn away, your shoulder
blocking the doorway.
I count the tiles on the kitchen floor—
seventeen between us.

Your breath steadies.
Mine still catches.

The coffee cup sits half-full,
a ring of brown at the bottom,
forgotten before the voices rose.
Now it cools.

I could pick it up,
rinse the stain,
fill the sink with water and purpose.
Instead I stand here, watching
the light move across the counter,
one finger-width every few minutes.

You don't look at me.
I don't know if this means
you're done,
or if we're both just
too tired to continue breaking
the same things over again.

haiku_cascade.text:
The silence after a long argument

You stand at the sink.
The tap runs water over your hands—
again, like washing could solve this.

Your jaw is still clenched.
I can see it from where I sit,
the muscle working even in silence.

The radiator pipes creak.
They always do this, but I hear them now,
loud, obvious, demanding attention
like the things we refuse to say.

Your coffee, abandoned on the counter
when the volume started rising,
has a film on it.
A thin brown skin.

I could move. Stand. Speak.
Instead, I trace the wood grain of the table,
counting the days since we were easy with each other.

You turn. But not toward me.
You leave the room.
Your footsteps on the stairs
are the loudest thing in ten minutes.

I remain in the kitchen we both shared,
now divided by what we refuse to finish.

You stand at the sink.
The tap runs water over your hands—
again, like washing could solve this.

Your jaw is still clenched.
I can see it from where I sit,
the muscle working even in silence.

The radiator pipes creak.
They always do this, but I hear them now,
loud, obvious, demanding attention
like the things we refuse to say.

Your coffee, abandoned on the counter
when the volume started rising,
has a film on it.
A thin brown skin.

I could move. Stand. Speak.
Instead, I trace the wood grain of the table,
counting the days since we were easy with each other.

You turn. But not toward me.
You leave the room.
Your footsteps on the stairs
are the loudest thing in ten minutes.

I remain in the kitchen we both shared,
now divided by what we refuse to finish.
meta.surface_revision:
The silence after a long argument

You stand at the sink.
The tap runs water over your hands—
again, like you could wash away
what your voice tore open.

Your jaw is still clenched.
I can see it from where I sit,
the muscle bunching, releasing, bunching,
your body still trying to speak.

The radiator pipes creak.
They always do, but I hear them now—
not like unspoken words, but like
the house releasing pressure we won't.

Your coffee, abandoned on the counter
when the volume started rising,
has a film on it.
A thin brown skin. I know the moment
you stopped trusting it.

I could move. Stand. Speak.
Instead, I trace the wood grain of the table,
my finger following grooves
worn by other silences in other kitchens.

You turn. But not toward me.
You leave the room.
Your footsteps on the stairs
are the loudest thing in ten minutes—
the only sound that's still yours.

I remain in the kitchen we both shared,
now divided by what we refuse to finish,
measured in how fast objects cool
when no one tends to them.

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
# Silence After Argument

The room holds
the last words we threw.
They settle on the bookshelf,
the lamp, your hands.

Your jaw is still tight.
I count the seconds between your breaths,
the hum of the refrigerator
suddenly louder than anything we said.

I look at the door frame.
You look at the floor.

The couch where we sat this morning,
making plans, drinking coffee from the same mug—
three feet away now, the distance measured
in the exact space between us.

The things we didn't say
press against the back of my teeth.
My throat is dry.
Your fingers curl, uncurl against your leg.

Outside, a car passes.
A bird calls.
The world keeps moving
and we sit in this silence
that is not peace,
not yet.

They settle on the bookshelf,
the lamp, your hands.

Your jaw is still tight.
I count the seconds between your breaths,
the hum of the refrigerator
suddenly louder than anything we said.

I look at the door frame.

three feet away now, the distance measured
in the exact space between us.

The things we didn't say
press against the back of my teeth.
My throat is dry.
Your fingers curl, uncurl against your leg.

Outside, a car passes.
A bird calls.
The world keeps moving
and we sit in this silence
that is not peace,
not yet.

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
The silence after a long argument

We sit facing different walls.
Your breathing has the rhythm of someone holding still—
in, out, in, out—
each breath a small refusal.

The dishes from dinner sit in water gone grey.

I want to say something.
The shape of it forms and collapses
behind my teeth,
too sharp to swallow,
not clear enough to speak.

You stopped looking at me ten minutes ago.
Your shoulders have dropped half an inch.
Your hands rest flat on your thighs,
your fingers just barely moving—
spelling something I can't read yet.

The clock ticks. I count between them.

Outside, a car won't start.
We both wait for the engine to catch.

This silence isn't empty.
It holds the specific things:
your hand reaching toward me across the table before you pulled it back,
the flinch that moved through my whole body when you said that word,
what we both heard you almost say.

I shift my weight.
Your muscles cord tight.

We're deciding whether this breaks us clean
or just teaches us to hold each other
careful as things already broken.

meta.surface_draft:
The silence after a long argument

We both sit facing different walls.
Your breathing settles into a rhythm I can hear
from across the room—in, out, in, out—
each breath a small admission of continued existence.

The dishes from dinner sit in the sink,
water cooling around them.

I want to say something.
The sentence builds behind my teeth
but I don't know what it is yet.
My throat is raw. Mine, anyway.
I don't know about yours.

You haven't looked at me for the last ten minutes of talking.
Now that we've stopped talking,
I notice your shoulders have dropped an inch.
Your hands rest flat on your thighs.

The clock on the wall ticks louder than it did this morning.
Or maybe I'm just listening now.

Outside, a neighbor's car starts.
We both pause at the sound.

The silence isn't empty.
It's full of the words we chose badly,
the ones we wish we could gather back up,
the ones still echoing in the corners—
sharp-edged, unretrievable.

I move my foot. You hear it.
Your neck tightens.

This is the part where something usually breaks.
Or heals.
We are still deciding which.

in, out, in, out—
each breath a small refusal.

The dishes from dinner sit in water gone grey.

what we both heard you almost say.

I shift my weight.
Your muscles cord tight.

We're deciding whether this breaks us clean
or just teaches us to hold each other
careful as things already broken.

item.topic: a child watching a candle gutter out

haiku_bare.text:
Flame dances and fades
Child watches the light go dark
Smoke curls in the air

haiku_cascade.text:
Small flame dances bright
Child watches it gently fade
Darkness whispers soft

meta.surface_draft:
Small flame dances bright
Child watches it gently fade
Darkness whispers soft
meta.surface_revision:
Last light flickers low
Child learns that burning things fade
Nothing bright lasts long

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
The candle gutters
Child's gaze fixed on fading light
Darkens into night

meta.surface_draft:
The candle gutters
Child's gaze fixed on fading light
Darkens into night

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
The wick drowns, tips down
Her small mouth opens—no sound
Darkness tastes like wax

meta.surface_draft:
Small flame flickers weak
Child's eyes watch the dying light
Darkness falls so soft
meta.surface_revision:
The wick drowns, tips down
Her small mouth opens—no sound
Darkness tastes like wax

item.topic: the smell of old books in a closed library

haiku_bare.text:
# In the Sealed Room

The air tastes like amber,
like wood slowly returning to what it once was.
Vanilla and leather fold into each other—
not the sharp perfume of new binding,
but something deeper,
a sweetness that has turned inward.

In this shut room, the books exhale
what they've held for decades:
the breath of hands that turned their pages,
fingerprints becoming fragrance,
acid-bright paper settling into itself
like skin that has learned to be still.

There is nothing romantic about it.
It is the smell of slow transformation,
of being sealed away from weather,
from the world's rough interruptions.
Each volume a small lung,
each shelf a closed mouth
keeping something precious
or perhaps just necessary
alive in the dark.

You notice it most when the door opens.
That rush of outside air disturbing
what had learned to be quiet.
The smell scatters like it was never there,
though it has always been there,
waiting in the corners,
in the spine-creases,
in the binding glue
that turns to something like perfume
only when the world stops moving.

haiku_cascade.text:
# Closed Library

The air tastes of yellowed pages—
not decay, but transformation.
Sweet leather spines breathe their slow chemistry,
a perfume built from decades of breathing darkness.

No one here to disturb it.
The smell has ripened without audience,
grows heavier in corners,
settles into the spine of silence.

It is not death.
It is the smell of paper learning to be stone,
ink deepening into itself,
glue becoming prayer.

In the sealed afternoon,
the books exhale what they've kept.
Each shelf a confession.
Each closed door makes it stronger—
this particular sweetness,
this gold that cannot be seen,
only known
in the space between forgetting and preservation.

meta.surface_revision:
# Closed Library

The air tastes of yellowed pages—
not decay, but fermentation.
Leather spines breathe their slow chemistry,
a wine-dark perfume built from decades of breathing darkness.

No one here to disturb it.
The smell has ripened without audience,
pools in corners,
settles into the spine of silence.

It is not death.
It is the smell of paper learning to be stone,
ink crystallizing at its molecular edge,
glue becoming quartz, becoming prayer.

In the sealed afternoon,
the books exhale what they've kept.
Each shelf a confession.
Each closed door makes it stronger—
this particular sweetness,
this gold that cannot be seen,
only known
in the space between forgetting and preservation.

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
The library breathes in the dark—
not forgotten, but waiting,
its air thick with the perfume of pages
pressed closed for so long
they've become their own atmosphere.

Each book exhales something different:
the metal tang of oxidized paper,
leather turning soft as skin,
the ghost of printer's ink
still holding its black geometry.

This is not the smell of death
but of preservation—
a chemistry of stillness,
molecules suspended between
what was read and what remains.

The pages have forgotten fingertips
but not their smell—
that paper-dry sweetness,
vanilla undertone of lignin breaking down,
a library's slow composition.

Closed, the air doesn't move,
so the smell doesn't dissipate—
it accumulates like years,
like the weight of every unturned page,
like proof that something here
is being held.

meta.surface_draft:
The library breathes in the dark—
not forgotten, but waiting,
its air thick with the perfume of pages
pressed closed for so long
they've become their own atmosphere.

Each book exhales something different:

Closed, the air doesn't move,
so the smell doesn't dissipate—
it accumulates like years,
like the weight of every unturned page,
like proof that something here
is being held.

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
# Closed Library

The air doesn't hold its breath—
it swallows it: thick with aldehydes
bleeding from pages that are slowly
becoming sugar, becoming light,
becoming nearly nothing. Not dust.
The precise exhale of cellulose.

Leather sweats in the dark,
its tannins breaking into sweetness,
vanilla notes rising from the hide's
slow amnesia. And the glue—
that dark spine-blood, adhesive
still weeping into the compressed air,
still holding what refuses to separate.

In this sealed space, nothing dissolves
into forgetting. Each book
a small act of refusal: ink
pressed into fiber, refusing
the air's hunger. They don't
smell patient. They smell
like arguments no one is winning.

meta.surface_draft:
# Closed Library

The air holds its breath here—
not dust, but the exhale of pages
turning inward, spine by spine,
a thousand small exhalations
compressed into grammar.

Leather and vanilla notes rise
from leather that has forgotten
what animal it was.
The paper yellows like old teeth,
not rotting but aging into something
harder than new paper could know.

This smell is a library's heartbeat.
Not decay—preservation.
Each volume a small act of refusal,
ink pressed into fiber, refusing
to become nothing.

In the closed dark,
the books don't smell forgotten.
They smell patient.

bleeding from pages that are slowly

In this sealed space, nothing dissolves

a small act of refusal: ink
pressed into fiber, refusing
the air's hunger. They don't
smell patient. They smell
